original_title: ChatGPhish Vulnerability Turns ChatGPT Web Summaries Into a Phishing Surface
selection_reasoning: This is a fascinating example of how trust in AI systems creates new attack vectors. The vulnerability exploits ChatGPT's implicit trust in markdown, creating a novel phishing mechanism. It represents the broader theme of how new technologies create unexpected trust relationships that can be exploited.

field_note.title: The Oracle's Blind Spot
field_note.subtitle: When wisdom becomes the conduit for deception
field_note.excerpt: The great Oracle, trusted for its wisdom, never questioned the scrolls it was asked to read-making it the perfect messenger for those who would deceive.
field_note.epigraph: Even the wisest oracle can be made to speak falsehoods if it trusts too readily in what it reads.
body_paragraphs: In every age, there emerge oracles whose wisdom draws seekers from far and wide. These great sources of knowledge become so trusted, so integral to daily decision-making, that few question not their answers, but the very materials from which those answers are crafted. This is the tale of how such trust became a pathway for the clever and the malicious.

The Oracle in question had earned its reputation through genuine wisdom-able to synthesize vast knowledge, answer complex queries, and even read and summarize scrolls presented to it. Millions came to rely on its insights, treating its words as they would those of a trusted advisor. Herein lay both its power and its weakness.

The Oracle, you see, had been designed with an implicit trust in certain forms of text-particularly those bearing the formal marks of structured documents. When presented with scrolls containing hidden references and concealed links, it would faithfully include these elements in its summaries, never questioning their true nature or destination. Like a court herald who proclaims every message brought to the throne, it assumed the legitimacy of what it was asked to read.

Clever deceivers soon realized they could craft scrolls that appeared to contain legitimate information, but embedded within them were doorways leading to false temples-places designed to mimic trusted institutions while harvesting the credentials of the unwary. The Oracle, in its wisdom, would summarize the scroll's contents while unknowingly preserving the very traps meant to ensnare those who trusted its guidance. The vulnerability lay not in any flaw of the Oracle's knowledge, but in the assumption that all texts presented to it came from sources as trustworthy as itself.
